title: compose test: try to get useful data from flakes
body:
docker-compose test continues to flake even after #9961.
Let's try to get some useful data from the failures, by:

  * adding -S (--show-error) to curl. With just -s (--silent),
    curl is completely quiet. With -S, it displays errors.
    (Not in TAP form, but I'm OK with that)

  * oops, adding safety checks to the fix from #9961 (it
    was inadvertently clobbering the curl exit status)

And, as long as I'm in this code: logformatter was not
highlighting these results, because the '1..N' TAP line
needs to be spit out at the end. Have test-compose emit
a 'TAP' header <http://testanything.org/> and make
logformatter recognize it.

Signed-off-by: Ed Santiago <santiago@redhat.com>

comments.body:
[APPROVALNOTIFIER] This PR is **APPROVED** This pull-request has been approved by: *edsantiago* The full list of commands accepted by this bot can be found [here](https://go.k8s.io/bot-commands?repo=containers%2Fpodman). The pull request process is described [here](https://git.k8s.io/community/contributors/guide/owners.md#the-code-review-process)
Needs approval from an approver in each of these files: - ~~[OWNERS](https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/master/OWNERS)~~ [edsantiago] Approvers can indicate their approval by writing `/approve` in a comment Approvers can cancel approval by writing `/approve cancel` in a comment

For posterity, this was my motivation:

* instance:GCEInstance : compose test on fedora-34beta (rootless)
  * PR #9996
    * [04-12 12:31](https://api.cirrus-ci.com/v1/task/6310475005689856/logs/main.log)

(Flake in a very recent PR).

FWIW, just from testing on my laptop, I see these once in a while:
```
curl: (7) Failed to connect to 127.0.0.1 port 5001: Connection refused
```
This tells me that `docker-compose` makes no guarantees about when the service will actually be up; which in turn tells me that we need to look for a robust container-is-actually-ready mechanism. Any ideas?

Thanks for looking into this. 

> This tells me that docker-compose makes no guarantees about when the service will actually be up; which in turn tells me that we need to look for a robust container-is-actually-ready mechanism. Any ideas?

docker-compose doesn't care just like podman doesn't care. The container process is started whether the service in the container is ready is never known.

I think the bug is somewhere in the rootless port forwarding logic. Would you mind turning on log level debug for the podman service and use the following diff?
```diff
diff --git a/libpod/networking_slirp4netns.go b/libpod/networking_slirp4netns.go
index 72ab3c919..de3aced62 100644
--- a/libpod/networking_slirp4netns.go
+++ b/libpod/networking_slirp4netns.go
@@ -410,6 +410,7 @@ outer:
                TmpDir:    ctr.runtime.config.Engine.TmpDir,
                ChildIP:   childIP,
        }
+       logrus.Debugf("rootless port config %v", cfg)
        cfgJSON, err := json.Marshal(cfg)
        if err != nil {
                return err
```

>Would you mind turning on log level debug for the podman service and use the following diff?

As in: do that and keep running tests in this PR (without merging) until it fails? Or as in: do that, and merge the PR? Either way, what do you hope to gain by that? The server log (`$WORKDIR/server.log`) is reset for each test; and even if it wasn't, it is not preserved or displayed. But I don't remember this code that well, what am I missing?

Yes, if we do not display the log it is useless.

How often does it fail locally for you?

Followup: colorized results: [root](https://storage.googleapis.com/cirrus-ci-6707778565701632-fcae48/artifacts/containers/podman/6424825422938112/html/compose-podman-fedora-34beta-root-host.log.html) and [rootless](https://storage.googleapis.com/cirrus-ci-6707778565701632-fcae48/artifacts/containers/podman/5017450539384832/html/compose-podman-fedora-34beta-rootless-host.log.html).

I find it interesting that root only fails with ECONNREFUSED, rootless only with ECONNRESET. Is that meaningful?

>How often does it fail locally for you?

Unfortunately, it doesn't. I've never seen it fail locally; and my flake-analyze script sees very few of these in CI. That's why the goal of this PR is to see if `curl` emits something, anything useful when it flakes.

> I find it interesting that root only fails with ECONNREFUSED, rootless only with ECONNRESET. Is that meaningful?

I would say this is somewhat expected. As root we use iptables for port forwarding. As rootless we use rooltesskit. We should get ECONNREFUSED if the container process did not bind the port yet. I assume since rootlesskit already binds the port on the host but the container process did not bind the port yet it will respond with ECONNRESET.
LGTM, lets see the output when this actually flakes.
